# Exodus Chapter 14 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~40v–41r (PDF pp. 76–77); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 54–55 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_014_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_014_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_014_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_014_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## YHWH Sets the Trap — The Command to Turn Back (vv. 1–4)

**1** And YHWH spoke to Mosheh, saying:

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְדַבֵּר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֵּאמֹר (vayedabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor). The standard divine-speech formula resumes. After the itinerary notice of 13:20 and the theophanic pillars of 13:21–22, YHWH now gives a specific tactical command — one that will appear, from the human perspective, to be a strategic blunder. LXX: καὶ ἐλάλησεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν λέγων.

**2** "Speak to the sons of Yisra'el, and let them turn back [ve-yashuvu] and encamp before Pi-ha-Chirot [Pi-hahiroth], between Migdol and between the sea, before Ba'al Tsefon [Baal-zephon] — opposite it you shall encamp by the sea.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads דַּבֵּר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְיָשֻׁבוּ וְיַחֲנוּ לִפְנֵי פִּי הַחִירֹת בֵּין מִגְדֹּל וּבֵין הַיָּם לִפְנֵי בַּעַל צְפֹן נִכְחוֹ תַחֲנוּ עַל־הַיָּם (dabber el-benei Yisra'el ve-yashuvu ve-yakhanu lifnei Pi ha-Chirot bein Migdol u-vein ha-yam lifnei Ba'al Tsefon nikhkho takhanu al-ha-yam, "speak to the sons of Yisra'el, and let them turn back and encamp before Pi-hahiroth, between Migdol and between the sea, before Baal-zephon — opposite it you shall encamp by the sea"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the commanded reversal:** The verb וְיָשֻׁבוּ (ve-yashuvu) = "and let them turn back" — from שׁוב (shuv, "to return, turn back"). YHWH commands Yisra'el to reverse direction, to double back toward Mitsrayim. This is not retreat but divine strategy — the turning back will lure Par'oh into pursuit and into the trap at the sea. **Place names:** פִּי הַחִירֹת (Pi ha-Chirot) = possibly "mouth of the gorges/canals" — from פִּי (pi, "mouth") + חִירֹת (Chirot, possibly from Egyptian meaning "canal" or "estate"). מִגְדֹּל (Migdol) = "tower, fortress" — from גדל (gadal, "to be great"); a common place-name element indicating a fortified outpost. בַּעַל צְפֹן (Ba'al Tsefon) = "Lord of the North" — a Canaanite divine epithet associated with a specific cultic site. The name בַּעַל (Ba'al, "lord, master") + צָפוֹן (tsafon, "north") may indicate a shrine to Ba'al on the northern coast. The geography pinpoints Yisra'el between Egyptian territory, the wilderness, and the sea — hemmed in on all sides. נִכְחוֹ (nikhkho) = "opposite it" — from נֹכַח (nokhakh, "in front of, opposite"). LXX: λάλησον τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ, καὶ ἀποστρέψαντες στρατοπεδευσάτωσαν ἀπέναντι τῆς ἐπαύλεως ἀνὰ μέσον Μαγδώλου καὶ ἀνὰ μέσον τῆς θαλάσσης ἐξ ἐναντίας Βεελσεπφων ("speak to the sons of Israel, and having turned back let them encamp before the farmstead, between Magdol and between the sea, opposite Beelsepphon") — ἔπαυλις ("farmstead, estate") for Pi-hahiroth — an interpretive rendering.

**3** And Par'oh will say concerning the sons of Yisra'el: 'They [are] wandering-in-confusion [nevukhim] in the land; the midbar [wilderness] has closed [sagar] upon them.'

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאָמַר פַּרְעֹה לִבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל נְבֻכִים הֵם בָּאָרֶץ סָגַר עֲלֵיהֶם הַמִּדְבָּר (ve-amar Par'oh li-vnei Yisra'el nevukhim hem ba-arets sagar aleihem ha-midbar, "and Par'oh will say concerning the sons of Yisra'el: 'wandering-in-confusion they [are] in the land; the wilderness has closed upon them'"). **Key terms:** נְבֻכִים (nevukhim) = "confused, perplexed, wandering aimlessly" — from the root בוך (bukh) or נבך (navakh), meaning "to be confused, entangled." The word appears only here and in Esther 3:15 and Joel 1:18. Par'oh interprets the reversal as disorientation — the slaves are lost, trapped, leaderless. The verb סָגַר (sagar) = "to close, shut in" — from סגר (sagar, "to shut, close"). The wilderness has "locked" them in — they are confined, trapped. This is precisely what YHWH intends Par'oh to think. The preposition לִ (li) before בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל may mean "concerning, about" (rather than "to"). LXX: καὶ ἐρεῖ Φαραω τῷ λαῷ αὐτοῦ· οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ πλανῶνται οὗτοι ἐν τῇ γῇ· συγκέκλεικεν γὰρ αὐτοὺς ἡ ἔρημος ("and Pharao will say to his people: 'the sons of Israel — these ones are wandering in the land; for the wilderness has shut them in'") — πλανάω ("wander, go astray") for nevukhim; συγκλείω ("shut in together") for sagar.

**4** And I will make strong [ve-khizzaqti] the lev [heart] of Par'oh, and he will pursue [ve-radaf] after them; and I will get glory [ve-ikkavdah] through Par'oh and through all his army [kheilo], and Mitsrayim will know that I [am] YHWH." And they did so.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְחִזַּקְתִּי אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְרָדַף אַחֲרֵיהֶם וְאִכָּבְדָה בְפַרְעֹה וּבְכָל־חֵילוֹ וְיָדְעוּ מִצְרַיִם כִּי־אֲנִי יְהוָה וַיַּעֲשׂוּ־כֵן (ve-khizzaqti et-lev Par'oh ve-radaf akhareihem ve-ikkavdah ve-Far'oh u-ve-khol-kheilo ve-yade'u Mitsrayim ki-ani YHWH vaya'asu-khen, "and I will make strong the heart of Par'oh, and he will pursue after them; and I will get glory through Par'oh and through all his army, and Mitsrayim will know that I [am] YHWH. And they did so"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the final hardening and the purpose of kavod:** The verb וְחִזַּקְתִּי (ve-khizzaqti) = piel of חזק (khazaq, "to be strong") — "I will make strong, I will harden." This is the decisive piel form with YHWH as subject — the active strengthening/hardening of Par'oh's resolve. After the plagues, Par'oh's heart is hardened one final time, not as an act of cruelty but as a setting of the stage for the supreme revelation at the sea. The verb וְאִכָּבְדָה (ve-ikkavdah) = niphal of כבד (kavad, "to be heavy, glorious") — "I will be glorified, I will get glory/honor for Myself." The root כבד appears throughout the plague narrative as "heavy/hard" (the hardening of the heart: כָּבֵד, kaved), but here in its niphal form it means "be honored, be glorified." The same root that described the weight of Par'oh's stubbornness now describes the weight of YHWH's glory — a deliberate wordplay. YHWH's kavod (glory/weight) will be demonstrated through Par'oh's destruction. The formula וְיָדְעוּ מִצְרַיִם כִּי־אֲנִי יְהוָה (ve-yade'u Mitsrayim ki-ani YHWH, "and Mitsrayim will know that I am YHWH") — the recognition formula, the theological goal of the entire exodus drama: that Egypt will know YHWH. LXX: καὶ ἐγὼ σκληρυνῶ τὴν καρδίαν Φαραω, καὶ καταδιώξεται ὀπίσω αὐτῶν· καὶ ἐνδοξασθήσομαι ἐν Φαραω καὶ ἐν πάσῃ τῇ στρατιᾷ αὐτοῦ, καὶ γνώσονται πάντες οἱ Αἰγύπτιοι ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι κύριος ("and I will harden the heart of Pharao, and he will pursue after them; and I will be glorified in Pharao and in all his army, and all the Egyptians will know that I am the Lord") — σκληρύνω ("harden") for khizzaqti; ἐνδοξάζομαι ("be glorified") for ikkavdah.

---

## Par'oh Pursues (vv. 5–9)

**5** And it was told to the melekh [king] of Mitsrayim that the people had fled [barakh]; and the levav [heart] of Par'oh and his servants was turned [vayeihafekh] toward the people, and they said: "What [is] this we have done, that we sent out Yisra'el from serving us [me-ovdenu]?"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֻּגַּד לְמֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם כִּי בָרַח הָעָם וַיֵּהָפֵךְ לְבַב פַּרְעֹה וַעֲבָדָיו אֶל־הָעָם וַיֹּאמְרוּ מַה־זֹּאת עָשִׂינוּ כִּי־שִׁלַּחְנוּ אֶת־יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵעָבְדֵנוּ (vayuggad le-melekh Mitsrayim ki varakh ha-am vayeihafekh levav Par'oh va-avadav el-ha-am vayomeru mah-zot asinu ki-shillakhnu et-Yisra'el me-ovdenu, "and it was told to the king of Mitsrayim that the people had fled; and the heart of Par'oh and his servants was turned toward the people, and they said: 'What is this we have done, that we sent out Yisra'el from serving us?'"). **Key terms:** The verb בָרַח (varakh) = "had fled" — from ברח (barakh, "to flee, run away"). Although Par'oh formally sent Yisra'el out (shillakh), now the departure is reframed as flight, as escape — the language of a slave-owner whose property has absconded. The verb וַיֵּהָפֵךְ (vayeihafekh) = niphal of הפך (hafakh, "to turn, overturn") — "was turned, was reversed." The heart of Par'oh "turned" — a reversal of his earlier decision to let them go. The phrase מֵעָבְדֵנוּ (me-ovdenu) = "from serving us" — from עבד (avad, "to serve, work, be enslaved"). Egypt's regret is economic: they have lost their slave labor force. The same root עבד that in 13:5 described sacred worship (avodah) here describes enslaved labor — the fundamental tension of the exodus: will Yisra'el serve (avad) YHWH or Par'oh? LXX: καὶ ἀνηγγέλη τῷ βασιλεῖ τῶν Αἰγυπτίων ὅτι πέφευγεν ὁ λαός· καὶ μετεστράφη ἡ καρδία Φαραω καὶ τῶν θεραπόντων αὐτοῦ ἐπὶ τὸν λαόν, καὶ εἶπαν· τί τοῦτο ἐποιήσαμεν τοῦ ἐξαποστεῖλαι τοὺς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ τοῦ μὴ δουλεύειν ἡμῖν; — μεταστρέφω ("turn about, reverse") for vayeihafekh.

**6** And he harnessed [vaye'sor] his chariot [rikhbo], and his people [ammo] he took with him.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֶּאְסֹר אֶת־רִכְבּוֹ וְאֶת־עַמּוֹ לָקַח עִמּוֹ (vaye'sor et-rikhbo ve-et-ammo laqakh immo, "and he harnessed his chariot, and his people he took with him"). The verb וַיֶּאְסֹר (vaye'sor) = qal of אסר (asar, "to bind, harness, tie") — the king himself harnesses his war-chariot. This personal action signals Par'oh's urgency and rage — he does not wait for servants. The word רֶכֶב (rekhev) = "chariot, chariotry" — singular here, referring to his personal royal chariot. LXX: ἔζευξεν οὖν τὰ ἅρματα αὐτοῦ καὶ πάντα τὸν λαὸν αὐτοῦ συναπήγαγεν μεθ᾽ ἑαυτοῦ ("he yoked his chariots and led away all his people with him") — ζεύγνυμι ("yoke, harness") for ye'sor.

**7** And he took shesh-me'ot [six hundred] chariot [rekhev] of the chosen [bakhur], and all the chariotry [rekhev] of Mitsrayim, and shalishim [officers/third-men] over all of it.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּקַּח שֵׁשׁ־מֵאוֹת רֶכֶב בָּחוּר וְכֹל רֶכֶב מִצְרָיִם וְשָׁלִשִׁם עַל־כֻּלּוֹ (vayiqqakh shesh-me'ot rekhev bakhur ve-khol rekhev Mitsrayim ve-shalishim al-kullo, "and he took six hundred chariot of the chosen, and all the chariotry of Mitsrayim, and officers over all of it"). **Key terms:** שֵׁשׁ־מֵאוֹת (shesh-me'ot) = "six hundred" — the elite strike force. בָּחוּר (bakhur) = "chosen, select" — from בחר (bakhar, "to choose, select"); these are the elite, hand-picked chariots. שָׁלִשִׁם (shalishim) = "officers, third-men" — from שָׁלֹשׁ (shalosh, "three"). The shalish was the third man on a chariot (driver, warrior, shield-bearer/officer), or more broadly a high-ranking military officer. The term became a general title for a senior military commander. LXX: καὶ λαβὼν ἑξακόσια ἅρματα ἐκλεκτὰ καὶ πᾶσαν τὴν ἵππον τῶν Αἰγυπτίων καὶ τριστάτας ἐπὶ πάντων ("and having taken six hundred chosen chariots and all the cavalry of the Egyptians and commanders over all") — ἐκλεκτός ("chosen, select") for bakhur; τριστάτης ("third-rank officer") for shalishim.

**8** And YHWH made strong [vayekhazeq] the lev [heart] of Par'oh, melekh [king] of Mitsrayim, and he pursued [vayirdof] after the sons of Yisra'el; and the sons of Yisra'el [were] going out be-yad ramah [with a high/uplifted hand].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְחַזֵּק יְהוָה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה מֶלֶךְ מִצְרַיִם וַיִּרְדֹּף אַחֲרֵי בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל יֹצְאִים בְּיָד רָמָה (vayekhazeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh melekh Mitsrayim vayirdof akharei benei Yisra'el u-venei Yisra'el yots'im be-yad ramah, "and YHWH made strong the heart of Par'oh, king of Mitsrayim, and he pursued after the sons of Yisra'el; and the sons of Yisra'el [were] going out with a high hand"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — beyad ramah:** The phrase בְּיָד רָמָה (be-yad ramah) = "with a high/uplifted hand" — from יָד (yad, "hand") + רָמָה (ramah, feminine of רָם, ram, "high, exalted"). The "high hand" is a gesture of defiance, triumph, and bold confidence — the raised fist of the liberated. Yisra'el goes out not skulking, not fleeing in shame, but with heads and hands held high. The image contrasts sharply with Par'oh's perception in v. 3 (nevukhim, "confused/wandering"). Numbers 15:30 uses the same phrase to describe willful, brazen sin — the "high hand" is an act of deliberate assertion. Here it is the assertion of freedom. LXX: καὶ ἐσκλήρυνεν κύριος τὴν καρδίαν Φαραω βασιλέως Αἰγύπτου, καὶ κατεδίωξεν ὀπίσω τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ· οἱ δὲ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ἐξεπορεύοντο ἐν χειρὶ ὑψηλῇ ("and the Lord hardened the heart of Pharao king of Egypt, and he pursued after the sons of Israel; and the sons of Israel were going out with a high hand") — χεὶρ ὑψηλή ("high hand") for yad ramah.

**9** And Mitsrayim pursued after them and overtook them — encamping by the sea — all the chariot-horses [sus rekhev] of Par'oh and his horsemen [parashav] and his army [kheilo] — by Pi-ha-Chirot, before Ba'al Tsefon.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּרְדְּפוּ מִצְרַיִם אַחֲרֵיהֶם וַיַּשִּׂיגוּ אוֹתָם חֹנִים עַל־הַיָּם כָּל־סוּס רֶכֶב פַּרְעֹה וּפָרָשָׁיו וְחֵילוֹ עַל־פִּי הַחִירֹת לִפְנֵי בַּעַל צְפֹן (vayirdefu Mitsrayim achareihem vayassigu otam khonim al-ha-yam kol-sus rekhev Par'oh u-farashav ve-kheilo al-Pi ha-Chirot lifnei Ba'al Tsefon, "and Mitsrayim pursued after them and overtook them — encamping by the sea — all the chariot-horses of Par'oh and his horsemen and his army — by Pi-hahiroth, before Baal-zephon"). **Key terms:** וַיַּשִּׂיגוּ (vayassigu) = hiphil of נשׂג / שׂוג (nasag, "to reach, overtake") — "they overtook them." The Egyptian army caught up to Yisra'el at the precise spot YHWH had commanded them to camp. The word חֹנִים (khonim) = "encamping" — participle of חנה (khanah, "to encamp"), indicating Yisra'el was stationary when the Egyptians arrived. סוּס (sus) = "horse" — the war-horse of Egypt, the military technology of the superpower. פָּרָשׁ (parash) = "horseman, rider" — cavalry. The military apparatus is listed in full: chariots, cavalry, infantry (kheil). LXX: καὶ κατεδίωξαν οἱ Αἰγύπτιοι ὀπίσω αὐτῶν καὶ εὕροσαν αὐτοὺς παρεμβεβληκότας παρὰ τὴν θάλασσαν... παρὰ τὴν ἔπαυλιν ἐξ ἐναντίας Βεελσεπφων ("and the Egyptians pursued after them and found them encamped by the sea... by the farmstead opposite Beelsepphon").

---

## The Terror of Yisra'el and the Response of Mosheh (vv. 10–14)

**10** And Par'oh drew near [hiqriv]; and the sons of Yisra'el lifted up their eyes, and behold — Mitsrayim [was] journeying after them; and they feared greatly [vayire'u me'od], and the sons of Yisra'el cried out [vayits'aqu] to YHWH.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּפַרְעֹה הִקְרִיב וַיִּשְׂאוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־עֵינֵיהֶם וְהִנֵּה מִצְרַיִם נֹסֵעַ אַחֲרֵיהֶם וַיִּירְאוּ מְאֹד וַיִּצְעֲקוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶל־יְהוָה (u-Far'oh hiqriv vayis'u venei-Yisra'el et-eineihem ve-hinneh Mitsrayim nosea achareihem vayire'u me'od vayits'aqu venei-Yisra'el el-YHWH, "and Par'oh drew near; and the sons of Yisra'el lifted up their eyes, and behold — Mitsrayim journeying after them; and they feared greatly, and the sons of Yisra'el cried out to YHWH"). **Key terms:** הִקְרִיב (hiqriv) = hiphil of קרב (qarav, "to come near") — Par'oh "caused nearness," he brought himself near, he advanced. The verb is active, aggressive. The singular נֹסֵעַ (nosea, "journeying") — Mitsrayim as a collective singular, the entire Egyptian force viewed as one mass bearing down. The verb וַיִּצְעֲקוּ (vayits'aqu) = "they cried out" — from צעק (tsa'aq, "to cry out, shout"). The same verb used in 2:23 for the cry of slavery — now it is the cry of terror. But crucially, they cry out אֶל־יְהוָה (el-YHWH, "to YHWH") — in their extremity, they turn to God. LXX: καὶ Φαραω προσῆγεν· καὶ ἀναβλέψαντες οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ τοῖς ὀφθαλμοῖς ὁρῶσιν, καὶ οἱ Αἰγύπτιοι ἐστρατοπέδευσαν ὀπίσω αὐτῶν· καὶ ἐφοβήθησαν σφόδρα, ἀνεβόησαν δὲ οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ πρὸς κύριον — ἀναβοάω ("cry aloud") for vayits'aqu.

**11** And they said to Mosheh: "Is it because there are no qevarim [graves] in Mitsrayim [that] you took us out to die in the midbar [wilderness]? What [is] this you have done to us, to bring us out [le-hotsi'anu] from Mitsrayim?

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמְרוּ אֶל־מֹשֶׁה הֲמִבְּלִי אֵין־קְבָרִים בְּמִצְרַיִם לְקַחְתָּנוּ לָמוּת בַּמִּדְבָּר מַה־זֹּאת עָשִׂיתָ לָּנוּ לְהוֹצִיאָנוּ מִמִּצְרָיִם (vayomeru el-Mosheh ha-mi-beli ein-qevarim be-Mitsrayim leqakhtanu lamut ba-midbar mah-zot asita lanu le-hotsi'anu mi-Mitsrayim, "and they said to Mosheh: 'Is it because there are no graves in Mitsrayim [that] you took us to die in the wilderness? What is this you have done to us, to bring us out from Mitsrayim?'"). **Key phrase:** הֲמִבְּלִי אֵין־קְבָרִים (ha-mi-beli ein-qevarim) = "Is it from lack of there being no graves?" — a double negative creating a bitterly ironic question. The construction is unusual and emphatic: מִבְּלִי (mi-beli) = "from a lack of" + אֵין (ein, "there is not") + קְבָרִים (qevarim, "graves"). Egypt, the land obsessed with tombs and burial, surely has graves enough! The sarcasm is bitter and pointed. This is the first of many wilderness complaints (cf. 15:24, 16:3, 17:2–3; Num 11:1, 14:2–4, 16:13–14, 20:3–5, 21:5). LXX: καὶ εἶπαν πρὸς Μωυσῆν· παρὰ τὸ μὴ ὑπάρχειν μνήματα ἐν γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ ἐξήγαγες ἡμᾶς θανατῶσαι ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ; τί τοῦτο ἐποίησας ἡμῖν ἐξαγαγὼν ἐξ Αἰγύπτου; — μνῆμα ("tomb, monument") for qevarim.

**12** Is not this the davar [word/thing] which we spoke to you in Mitsrayim, saying: 'Leave us alone [khadal], and let us serve [ve-na'avdah] Mitsrayim'? For [it is] better for us to serve [avod] Mitsrayim than our dying in the midbar [wilderness]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads הֲלֹא־זֶה הַדָּבָר אֲשֶׁר דִּבַּרְנוּ אֵלֶיךָ בְמִצְרַיִם לֵאמֹר חֲדַל מִמֶּנּוּ וְנַעַבְדָה אֶת־מִצְרָיִם כִּי טוֹב לָנוּ עֲבֹד אֶת־מִצְרָיִם מִמֻּתֵנוּ בַּמִּדְבָּר (halo-zeh ha-davar asher dibbanu elekha ve-Mitsrayim lemor khadal mimmenu ve-na'avdah et-Mitsrayim ki tov lanu avod et-Mitsrayim mi-mutenu ba-midbar, "is not this the word which we spoke to you in Mitsrayim, saying: 'Leave us alone and let us serve Mitsrayim'? For good for us [is] serving Mitsrayim more than our dying in the wilderness"). **Key terms:** The verb חֲדַל (khadal) = "cease, desist, leave off" — from חדל (khadal, "to cease, stop"). "Stop bothering us, leave us be." The phrase וְנַעַבְדָה (ve-na'avdah) = "and let us serve" — cohortative of עבד (avad, "to serve"). The people prefer slavery to death. This verse references a conversation implied in 5:21 and 6:9, where the people rejected Mosheh's intervention. The theological tragedy: the people have been delivered from Par'oh's hand but not yet from Par'oh's mentality. The slave-consciousness prefers the known bondage to the unknown freedom. LXX: οὐ τοῦτο ἦν τὸ ῥῆμα ὃ ἐλαλήσαμεν πρὸς σὲ ἐν Αἰγύπτῳ λέγοντες· πάρες ἡμᾶς ὅπως δουλεύσωμεν τοῖς Αἰγυπτίοις; κρεῖσσον γὰρ ἡμᾶς δουλεύειν τοῖς Αἰγυπτίοις ἢ ἀποθανεῖν ἐν τῇ ἐρήμῳ ταύτῃ.

**13** And Mosheh said to the people: "Do not fear [al-tire'u]! Stand firm [hityatsavu] and see [u-re'u] the yeshu'at [salvation of] YHWH which He will do for you today; for as you have seen Mitsrayim today, you will not add [lo tosifu] to see them again, unto olam [forever].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה אֶל־הָעָם אַל־תִּירָאוּ הִתְיַצְּבוּ וּרְאוּ אֶת־יְשׁוּעַת יְהוָה אֲשֶׁר־יַעֲשֶׂה לָכֶם הַיּוֹם כִּי אֲשֶׁר רְאִיתֶם אֶת־מִצְרַיִם הַיּוֹם לֹא תֹסִפוּ לִרְאֹתָם עוֹד עַד־עוֹלָם (vayomer Mosheh el-ha-am al-tire'u hityatsavu u-re'u et-yeshu'at YHWH asher-ya'aseh lakhem ha-yom ki asher re'item et-Mitsrayim ha-yom lo tosifu lir'otam od ad-olam, "and Mosheh said to the people: 'Do not fear! Stand firm and see the salvation of YHWH which He will do for you today; for as you have seen Mitsrayim today, you will not add to see them again, unto forever'"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the command to stand and see:** Three imperatives define Yisra'el's role: (1) אַל־תִּירָאוּ (al-tire'u) = "do not fear!" — jussive negative, a prohibition against the terror of v. 10; (2) הִתְיַצְּבוּ (hityatsavu) = "stand firm, station yourselves" — hitpael of יצב (yatsav, "to set, station oneself"), meaning "take your stand, plant yourselves"; (3) וּרְאוּ (u-re'u) = "and see!" — imperative of ראה (ra'ah, "to see"). The people are commanded to do nothing except stand and watch. The deliverance is YHWH's alone. The word יְשׁוּעָה (yeshu'ah) = "salvation, deliverance" — from ישׁע (yasha, "to save, deliver"). This is YHWH's yeshu'ah, not Yisra'el's military victory. The phrase לֹא תֹסִפוּ לִרְאֹתָם (lo tosifu lir'otam) = "you will not add to see them" = "you will never again see them" — the Egyptian army will be permanently destroyed. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ Μωυσῆς πρὸς τὸν λαόν· θαρσεῖτε· στῆτε καὶ ὁρᾶτε τὴν σωτηρίαν τὴν παρὰ τοῦ θεοῦ, ἣν ποιήσει ἡμῖν σήμερον... οὐ προσθήσεσθε ἔτι ἰδεῖν αὐτοὺς εἰς τὸν αἰῶνα χρόνον — θαρσέω ("take courage") for al-tire'u; σωτηρία ("salvation") for yeshu'ah.

**14** YHWH will fight [yillakhem] for you, and you — you shall be silent [takharishun]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads יְהוָה יִלָּחֵם לָכֶם וְאַתֶּם תַּחֲרִישׁוּן (YHWH yillakhem lakhem ve-attem takharishun, "YHWH will fight for you, and you — you shall be silent"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — divine warfare and human silence:** The verb יִלָּחֵם (yillakhem) = niphal of לחם (lakham, "to fight, wage war") — "will fight." YHWH is the warrior; the battle is His. The verb תַּחֲרִישׁוּן (takharishun) = hiphil of חרשׁ (kharash, "to be silent, to hold one's peace") — "you shall be silent / still / quiet." The unusual long form with final -un (pausal/archaic). The silence commanded is total: no battle cry, no military action, no human intervention. Yisra'el's only task is to stand, see, and be silent. The verse is one of the most concentrated theological statements in the Hebrew Bible: YHWH fights; humanity is silent. The theological pattern: salvation is not human achievement but divine act. LXX: κύριος πολεμήσει περὶ ὑμῶν, καὶ ὑμεῖς σιγήσετε ("the Lord will fight for you, and you shall be silent") — πολεμέω ("wage war") for yillakhem; σιγάω ("be silent") for takharishun.

## YHWH's Command: Divide the Sea (vv. 15–18)

**15** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Why do you cry out [tits'aq] to Me? Speak to the sons of Yisra'el, and let them journey [ve-yissa'u]!

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה מַה־תִּצְעַק אֵלָי דַּבֵּר אֶל־בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל וְיִסָּעוּ (vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh mah-tits'aq elai dabber el-benei Yisra'el ve-yissa'u, "and YHWH said to Mosheh: 'Why do you cry out to Me? Speak to the sons of Yisra'el, and let them journey!'"). **Key dynamic:** YHWH's response to Mosheh is abrupt, almost impatient: מַה־תִּצְעַק (mah-tits'aq) = "why are you crying out?" — implying Mosheh had been praying or pleading. The time for prayer has passed; now is the time for action. The imperative וְיִסָּעוּ (ve-yissa'u) = "let them journey / set out" — from נסע (nasa, "to pull up, journey"). The command is forward: not back, not standing still, but forward — into the sea. LXX: καὶ εἶπεν κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν· τί βοᾷς πρός με; λάλησον τοῖς υἱοῖς Ισραηλ, καὶ ἀναζευξάτωσαν — βοάω ("cry out") for tits'aq; ἀναζεύγνυμι ("break camp, march") for yissa'u.

**16** And you — lift up [harem] your matteh [rod/staff] and stretch out [u-neteh] your hand over the sea and divide it [u-veqa'ehu]; and the sons of Yisra'el shall go [ve-yavo'u] into the midst of the sea on dry ground [ba-yabbashah].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְאַתָּה הָרֵם אֶת־מַטְּךָ וּנְטֵה אֶת־יָדְךָ עַל־הַיָּם וּבְקָעֵהוּ וְיָבֹאוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּתוֹךְ הַיָּם בַּיַּבָּשָׁה (ve-attah harem et-mattekha u-neteh et-yadkha al-ha-yam u-veqa'ehu ve-yavo'u venei-Yisra'el be-tokh ha-yam ba-yabbashah, "and you — lift up your rod and stretch out your hand over the sea and divide it; and the sons of Yisra'el shall go into the midst of the sea on dry ground"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the splitting of the sea:** Three commands to Mosheh: (1) הָרֵם (harem) = "lift up!" — hiphil imperative of רום (rum, "to be high, exalted"); (2) וּנְטֵה (u-neteh) = "and stretch out!" — imperative of נטה (natah, "to stretch out, extend"); (3) וּבְקָעֵהוּ (u-veqa'ehu) = "and split it!" — imperative of בקע (baqa, "to split, cleave, break open") + 3ms suffix. The verb בקע is the key verb of the sea narrative — it means to split, rend, cleave apart, break through. It is used for splitting wood (Eccl 10:9), splitting rocks (Isa 48:21), and here for splitting the sea. The matteh is the rod of divine power (cf. 4:2–4, 7:9–12, 17:5–6). The word יַבָּשָׁה (yabbashah) = "dry ground" — the sea-bed will become land. The impossible is commanded: the sea will become a road. LXX: καὶ σὺ ἔπαρον τῇ ῥάβδῳ σου καὶ ἔκτεινον τὴν χεῖρά σου ἐπὶ τὴν θάλασσαν καὶ ῥῆξον αὐτήν, καὶ εἰσελθάτωσαν οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ εἰς μέσον τῆς θαλάσσης κατὰ τὸ ξηρόν — ῥήγνυμι ("tear, rend, break") for baqa; ξηρόν ("dry [ground]") for yabbashah.

**17** And I — behold, I [am] making strong [mekhazeq] the lev [heart] of Mitsrayim, and they shall come in [ve-yavo'u] after them; and I will get glory [ve-ikkavdah] through Par'oh and through all his army [kheilo], through his chariotry [rikhbo] and through his horsemen [parashav].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַאֲנִי הִנְנִי מְחַזֵּק אֶת־לֵב מִצְרַיִם וְיָבֹאוּ אַחֲרֵיהֶם וְאִכָּבְדָה בְּפַרְעֹה וּבְכָל־חֵילוֹ בְּרִכְבּוֹ וּבְפָרָשָׁיו (va-ani hineni mekhazeq et-lev Mitsrayim ve-yavo'u achareihem ve-ikkavdah be-Far'oh u-ve-khol-kheilo be-rikhbo u-ve-farashav, "and I — behold, I [am] making strong the heart of Mitsrayim, and they shall come in after them; and I will get glory through Par'oh and through all his army, through his chariotry and through his horsemen"). The phrase הִנְנִי מְחַזֵּק (hineni mekhazeq) = "behold, I [am] making strong" — the hineni + participle construction indicates imminent, ongoing action. YHWH is in the process of hardening — not only Par'oh's heart but the heart of all Mitsrayim. The repetition of ve-ikkavdah from v. 4 underscores the purpose: divine glory through divine judgment. The military catalog — kheil (army), rekhev (chariotry), parashim (horsemen) — lists the full apparatus that will be destroyed. LXX: ἐγὼ δὲ ἰδοὺ σκληρυνῶ τὴν καρδίαν Φαραω καὶ τῶν Αἰγυπτίων πάντων, καὶ εἰσελεύσονται ὀπίσω αὐτῶν· καὶ ἐνδοξασθήσομαι ἐν Φαραω καὶ ἐν πάσῃ τῇ στρατιᾷ αὐτοῦ καὶ ἐπὶ τοῖς ἅρμασιν καὶ ἐπὶ τοῖς ἵπποις αὐτοῦ.

**18** And Mitsrayim shall know that I [am] YHWH, when I get glory [be-hikkavdi] through Par'oh, through his chariotry [rikhbo] and through his horsemen [parashav]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְיָדְעוּ מִצְרַיִם כִּי־אֲנִי יְהוָה בְּהִכָּבְדִי בְּפַרְעֹה בְּרִכְבּוֹ וּבְפָרָשָׁיו (ve-yade'u Mitsrayim ki-ani YHWH be-hikkavdi be-Far'oh be-rikhbo u-ve-farashav, "and Mitsrayim shall know that I [am] YHWH, when I get glory through Par'oh, through his chariotry and through his horsemen"). The recognition formula again (cf. v. 4): the goal is knowledge — not mere destruction but revelation. The preposition בְּ (be) with hikkavdi is instrumental: "by means of My being glorified in/through Par'oh." Egypt's most powerful military forces become the medium of YHWH's self-revelation. LXX: καὶ γνώσονται πάντες οἱ Αἰγύπτιοι ὅτι ἐγώ εἰμι κύριος, ἐνδοξαζομένου μου ἐν Φαραω καὶ ἐν τοῖς ἅρμασιν καὶ ἵπποις αὐτοῦ.

## The Angel, the Pillar, and the Darkness (vv. 19–20)

**19** And the mal'akh [angel/messenger] of ha-Elohim [God], the one going before the makhaneh [camp] of Yisra'el, journeyed and went behind them; and the ammud [pillar] of the anan [cloud] journeyed from before them and stood behind them.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּסַּע מַלְאַךְ הָאֱלֹהִים הַהֹלֵךְ לִפְנֵי מַחֲנֵה יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיֵּלֶךְ מֵאַחֲרֵיהֶם וַיִּסַּע עַמּוּד הֶעָנָן מִפְּנֵיהֶם וַיַּעֲמֹד מֵאַחֲרֵיהֶם (vayissa mal'akh ha-Elohim ha-holekh lifnei makhaneh Yisra'el vayelekh me-achareihem vayissa ammud he-anan mi-peneihem vaya'amod me-achareihem, "and the angel/messenger of God, the one going before the camp of Yisra'el, journeyed and went behind them; and the pillar of the cloud journeyed from before them and stood behind them"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the mal'akh and the pillar:** The מַלְאַךְ הָאֱלֹהִים (mal'akh ha-Elohim) = "angel/messenger of God" — from מַלְאָךְ (mal'akh, "messenger, angel") + הָאֱלֹהִים (ha-Elohim, "God"). In 13:21 it was YHWH Himself going before them; now it is the mal'akh ha-Elohim. The relationship between YHWH and His mal'akh is fluid in the Pentateuch — the mal'akh both represents and is YHWH (cf. Gen 16:7–13, 22:11–15, Exod 3:2–4). The angel and the pillar of cloud both execute the same movement: from the front (leading position) to the rear (protective position). The camp of Yisra'el is now shielded from behind — the Egyptian pursuit is blocked. The word מַחֲנֶה (makhaneh) = "camp" — from חנה (khanah, "to encamp"); the organized encampment of Yisra'el as a military-sacral body. LXX: ἐξῆρεν δὲ ὁ ἄγγελος τοῦ θεοῦ ὁ προπορευόμενος τῆς παρεμβολῆς τῶν υἱῶν Ισραηλ καὶ ἐπορεύθη ἐκ τῶν ὄπισθεν· ἐξῆρεν δὲ καὶ ὁ στῦλος τῆς νεφέλης ἀπὸ προσώπου αὐτῶν καὶ ἔστη ἐκ τῶν ὀπίσω αὐτῶν — ἄγγελος τοῦ θεοῦ ("angel of God") for mal'akh ha-Elohim.

**20** And it came between the makhaneh [camp] of Mitsrayim and between the makhaneh [camp] of Yisra'el; and there was the anan [cloud] and the khoshekh [darkness], and it illuminated [vaya'er] the lailah [night]; and one did not come near [lo-qarav] the other all the lailah [night].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּבֹא בֵּין מַחֲנֵה מִצְרַיִם וּבֵין מַחֲנֵה יִשְׂרָאֵל וַיְהִי הֶעָנָן וְהַחֹשֶׁךְ וַיָּאֶר אֶת־הַלָּיְלָה וְלֹא־קָרַב זֶה אֶל־זֶה כָּל־הַלָּיְלָה (vayavo bein makhaneh Mitsrayim u-vein makhaneh Yisra'el vayehi he-anan ve-ha-khoshekh vaya'er et-ha-lailah ve-lo-qarav zeh el-zeh kol-ha-lailah, "and it came between the camp of Mitsrayim and between the camp of Yisra'el; and there was the cloud and the darkness, and it illuminated the night; and one did not come near the other all the night"). **Key terms:** The pillar of cloud now functions as a barrier, a wall of separation between the two camps. The phrase וַיְהִי הֶעָנָן וְהַחֹשֶׁךְ (vayehi he-anan ve-ha-khoshekh) = "and there was the cloud and the darkness" — on the Egyptian side, the cloud brought darkness; the verb וַיָּאֶר (vaya'er) = hiphil of אור (or, "to give light, illuminate") — on the Israelite side, it gave light. The cloud is simultaneously darkness for the Egyptians and light for Yisra'el — an echo of the ninth plague (10:21–23), where darkness covered Mitsrayim but Yisra'el had light. The phrase וְלֹא־קָרַב זֶה אֶל־זֶה (ve-lo-qarav zeh el-zeh) = "and one did not come near the other" — complete separation through the night. LXX: καὶ εἰσῆλθεν ἀνὰ μέσον τῆς παρεμβολῆς τῶν Αἰγυπτίων καὶ ἀνὰ μέσον τῆς παρεμβολῆς Ισραηλ· καὶ ἔστη· καὶ ἐγένετο σκότος καὶ γνόφος, καὶ διῆλθεν ἡ νύξ, καὶ οὐ συνέμιξαν ἀλλήλοις ὅλην τὴν νύκτα ("and it entered between the camp of the Egyptians and the camp of Israel; and it stood; and there was darkness and gloom, and the night passed, and they did not mingle with each other all the night") — **textual note:** LXX reads σκότος καὶ γνόφος ("darkness and gloom") and lacks the MT's reference to illumination — a significant variant. The LXX emphasizes darkness on both sides rather than the MT's dual function (darkness for Egypt, light for Israel).

## The Splitting of the Sea (vv. 21–22)

**21** And Mosheh stretched out [vayyet] his hand over the sea; and YHWH caused the sea to go [vayolekh] by a ruach qadim azzah [strong east wind] all the lailah [night], and He set [vayasem] the sea to kharavah [dry ground], and the waters were divided [vayibbaq'u].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֵּט מֹשֶׁה אֶת־יָדוֹ עַל־הַיָּם וַיּוֹלֶךְ יְהוָה אֶת־הַיָּם בְּרוּחַ קָדִים עַזָּה כָּל־הַלַּיְלָה וַיָּשֶׂם אֶת־הַיָּם לֶחָרָבָה וַיִּבָּקְעוּ הַמָּיִם (vayyet Mosheh et-yado al-ha-yam vayolekh YHWH et-ha-yam be-ruakh qadim azzah kol-ha-lailah vayasem et-ha-yam le-kharavah vayibbaq'u ha-mayim, "and Mosheh stretched out his hand over the sea; and YHWH caused the sea to go by a strong east wind all the night, and He set the sea to dry ground, and the waters were divided"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the mechanism of the miracle:** The verse layers human action and divine agency: Mosheh stretches out his hand (the human instrument), but YHWH causes the sea to move (the divine agent). The instrument: רוּחַ קָדִים עַזָּה (ruakh qadim azzah) = "a strong east wind" — רוּחַ (ruakh) = "wind/spirit/breath"; קָדִים (qadim) = "east, eastern" — from קֶדֶם (qedem, "east, front, ancient"); עַזָּה (azzah) = "strong, fierce" — feminine of עַז (az, "strong"). The east wind is the hot, desiccating wind from the desert — the sirocco/khamsin. The same ruakh that hovered over the waters in Bereshit 1:2 now divides them. The verb וַיּוֹלֶךְ (vayolekh) = hiphil of הלך (halakh, "to walk, go") — "He caused [the sea] to go/move." The verb וַיָּשֶׂם (vayasem) = "He set, placed" — from שׂים (sim, "to set, place"). YHWH "set" the sea to dry ground — a creative act, transforming water into land, as in the creation narrative (Gen 1:9). The verb וַיִּבָּקְעוּ (vayibbaq'u) = niphal of בקע (baqa, "to split, cleave") — "were split, were divided." The fulfillment of the command in v. 16. The word חָרָבָה (kharavah) = "dry ground, dried up land" — from חרב (kharav, "to be dry, be desolate"). LXX: ἐξέτεινεν δὲ Μωυσῆς τὴν χεῖρα ἐπὶ τὴν θάλασσαν· καὶ ὑπήγαγεν κύριος τὴν θάλασσαν ἐν ἀνέμῳ νότῳ βιαίῳ ὅλην τὴν νύκτα καὶ ἐποίησεν τὴν θάλασσαν ξηράν, καὶ ἐσχίσθη τὸ ὕδωρ ("and Mosheh stretched out his hand over the sea; and the Lord drove back the sea by a violent south wind all the night and made the sea dry, and the water was divided") — **textual note:** LXX reads ἄνεμος νότος ("south wind") where MT reads ruakh qadim ("east wind") — a significant geographical variant. σχίζω ("split, cleave") for baqa.

**22** And the sons of Yisra'el went [vayavo'u] into the midst of the sea on the yabbashah [dry ground]; and the waters [were] to them a khomah [wall] on their right [mi-yeminam] and on their left [u-mi-semolam].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּבֹאוּ בְנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל בְּתוֹךְ הַיָּם בַּיַּבָּשָׁה וְהַמַּיִם לָהֶם חוֹמָה מִימִינָם וּמִשְּׂמֹאלָם (vayavo'u venei-Yisra'el be-tokh ha-yam ba-yabbashah ve-ha-mayim lahem khomah mi-yeminam u-mi-semolam, "and the sons of Yisra'el went into the midst of the sea on the dry ground; and the waters [were] to them a wall on their right and on their left"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the walls of water:** The word חוֹמָה (khomah) = "wall" — from חמה or חום, a fortified wall, a city wall. The waters stand upright like the walls of a city, creating a corridor of dry ground. The image is architectural: Yisra'el walks between two vertical walls of water, as through a fortified passage. The phrase מִימִינָם וּמִשְּׂמֹאלָם (mi-yeminam u-mi-semolam) = "on their right and on their left" — יָמִין (yamin, "right") + שְׂמֹאל (semol, "left"). The dry ground בַּיַּבָּשָׁה (ba-yabbashah) is the sea-bed transformed — water above, water on both sides, dry earth beneath. This is a reversal of creation's separation of waters (Gen 1:6–10) applied locally for salvation. LXX: καὶ εἰσῆλθον οἱ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ εἰς μέσον τῆς θαλάσσης κατὰ τὸ ξηρόν, καὶ τὸ ὕδωρ αὐτοῖς τεῖχος ἐκ δεξιῶν καὶ τεῖχος ἐξ εὐωνύμων — τεῖχος ("wall") for khomah.

## The Egyptian Pursuit into the Sea (vv. 23–25)

**23** And Mitsrayim pursued and came in after them — all the horses [sus] of Par'oh, his chariotry [rikhbo] and his horsemen [parashav] — into the midst of the sea.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּרְדְּפוּ מִצְרַיִם וַיָּבֹאוּ אַחֲרֵיהֶם כֹּל סוּס פַּרְעֹה רִכְבּוֹ וּפָרָשָׁיו אֶל־תּוֹךְ הַיָּם (vayirdefu Mitsrayim vayavo'u achareihem kol sus Par'oh rikhbo u-farashav el-tokh ha-yam, "and Mitsrayim pursued and came in after them — all the horses of Par'oh, his chariotry and his horsemen — into the midst of the sea"). The Egyptian army follows Yisra'el into the corridor between the walls of water — the trap is sprung. The full military catalog again: sus (horses), rekhev (chariotry), parashim (horsemen). They enter the sea voluntarily, driven by their hardened hearts. LXX: κατεδίωξαν δὲ οἱ Αἰγύπτιοι καὶ εἰσῆλθον ὀπίσω αὐτῶν, πᾶσα ἵππος Φαραω καὶ τὰ ἅρματα καὶ οἱ ἀναβάται, εἰς μέσον τῆς θαλάσσης.

**24** And it was in the ashmoret [watch] of the morning [ha-boqer], and YHWH looked down [vayashqef] at the makhaneh [camp] of Mitsrayim in a pillar of fire and cloud [be-ammud esh va-anan], and He confused [vayaham] the makhaneh [camp] of Mitsrayim.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיְהִי בְּאַשְׁמֹרֶת הַבֹּקֶר וַיַּשְׁקֵף יְהוָה אֶל־מַחֲנֵה מִצְרַיִם בְּעַמּוּד אֵשׁ וְעָנָן וַיָּהָם אֵת מַחֲנֵה מִצְרַיִם (vayehi be-ashmoret ha-boqer vayashqef YHWH el-makhaneh Mitsrayim be-ammud esh va-anan vayaham et makhaneh Mitsrayim, "and it was in the watch of the morning, and YHWH looked down at the camp of Mitsrayim in a pillar of fire and cloud, and He confused the camp of Mitsrayim"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the divine gaze:** The word אַשְׁמֹרֶת (ashmoret) = "watch" — from שׁמר (shamar, "to keep, guard, watch"). The night was divided into watches; the ashmoret ha-boqer ("morning watch") is the last watch of the night, the pre-dawn hours — the darkest hour before dawn. The verb וַיַּשְׁקֵף (vayashqef) = hiphil of שׁקף (shaqaf, "to look down, gaze out, peer") — YHWH "looked down" from the pillar of fire and cloud. The divine gaze is not passive observation but active intervention — when YHWH looks, things happen (cf. Gen 18:16, 19:28; Ps 14:2). The pillar now combines both elements — esh (fire) and anan (cloud) together, in the pre-dawn moment where night and day overlap. The verb וַיָּהָם (vayaham) = qal of הום/המם (hamam, "to throw into confusion, confuse, rout, panic") — "He confused, He threw into panic." The same verb describes YHWH's intervention in battle in Josh 10:10, Judg 4:15, 1 Sam 7:10. The divine panic — a supernatural confusion that shatters military cohesion. LXX: ἐγενήθη δὲ ἐν τῇ φυλακῇ τῇ ἑωθινῇ καὶ ἐπέβλεψεν κύριος ἐπὶ τὴν παρεμβολὴν τῶν Αἰγυπτίων ἐν στύλῳ πυρὸς καὶ νεφέλης καὶ συνετάραξεν τὴν παρεμβολὴν τῶν Αἰγυπτίων ("and it was in the morning watch, and the Lord looked upon the camp of the Egyptians in a pillar of fire and cloud, and threw into confusion the camp of the Egyptians") — ἐπιβλέπω ("look upon") for vayashqef; συνταράσσω ("throw into disorder") for vayaham.

**25** And He removed [vayasar] the wheel [ofan] of his chariots [markevotav], and He drove them [vayenahagehu] with heaviness [bi-khvedut]; and Mitsrayim said: "Let me flee [anusah] from the face of Yisra'el, for YHWH [is] fighting [nilkham] for them against Mitsrayim!"

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּסַר אֵת אֹפַן מַרְכְּבֹתָיו וַיְנַהֲגֵהוּ בִּכְבֵדֻת וַיֹּאמֶר מִצְרַיִם אָנוּסָה מִפְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל כִּי יְהוָה נִלְחָם לָהֶם בְּמִצְרָיִם (vayasar et ofan markevotav vayenahagehu bi-khvedut vayomer Mitsrayim anusah mi-penei Yisra'el ki YHWH nilkham lahem be-Mitsrayim, "and He removed the wheel of his chariots, and He drove them with heaviness; and Mitsrayim said: 'Let me flee from the face of Yisra'el, for YHWH is fighting for them against Mitsrayim!'"). **Key terms:** The verb וַיָּסַר (vayasar) = hiphil of סור (sur, "to turn aside, remove") — "He removed, He caused to come off." **Textual note:** The MT reading וַיָּסַר (vayasar, "He removed") is supported by many witnesses; however, some manuscripts and the Samaritan Pentateuch read וַיָּסַט (vayasat) or the Qere tradition may vary. The Septuagint reads συνέδησεν (sunedesen, "He bound, clogged"), suggesting a different Vorlage — perhaps ויאסר ("He bound"). The word אֹפַן (ofan) = "wheel" — the chariot wheels were removed or locked, rendering the vehicles useless in the muddy sea-bed. The phrase בִּכְבֵדֻת (bi-khvedut) = "with heaviness, with difficulty" — from כבד (kavad, "to be heavy"). The root כבד again: the same "heaviness" that hardened Par'oh's heart now weighs down his chariots. The verb וַיְנַהֲגֵהוּ (vayenahagehu) = piel of נהג (nahag, "to drive, lead") — "He drove them" — the chariots lurched forward under divine compulsion, heavy, dragging, uncontrollable. The cry אָנוּסָה (anusah) = "let me flee!" — cohortative of נוס (nus, "to flee"). Egypt recognizes too late that YHWH fights for Yisra'el — the very statement Mosheh had made in v. 14 (YHWH yillakhem lakhem) now comes from Egypt's own mouth, but as a cry of terror rather than faith. LXX: καὶ συνέδησεν τοὺς ἄξονας τῶν ἁρμάτων αὐτῶν καὶ ἤγαγεν αὐτοὺς μετὰ βίας· καὶ εἶπαν οἱ Αἰγύπτιοι· φύγωμεν ἀπὸ προσώπου Ισραηλ· ὁ γὰρ κύριος πολεμεῖ περὶ αὐτῶν τοὺς Αἰγυπτίους ("and He bound the axles of their chariots and drove them with force; and the Egyptians said: 'Let us flee from the face of Israel; for the Lord fights for them against the Egyptians'") — **textual note:** LXX reads ἄξων ("axle") rather than MT's ofan ("wheel"), and συνδέω ("bind") rather than sur ("remove") — suggesting the chariots were clogged/bound rather than their wheels removed.

## The Return of the Waters (vv. 26–28)

**26** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Stretch out [neteh] your hand over the sea, and the waters shall return [ve-yashuvu] upon Mitsrayim, upon his chariotry [rikhbo] and upon his horsemen [parashav]."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה נְטֵה אֶת־יָדְךָ עַל־הַיָּם וְיָשֻׁבוּ הַמַּיִם עַל־מִצְרַיִם עַל־רִכְבּוֹ וְעַל־פָּרָשָׁיו (vayomer YHWH el-Mosheh neteh et-yadkha al-ha-yam ve-yashuvu ha-mayim al-Mitsrayim al-rikhbo ve-al-parashav, "and YHWH said to Mosheh: 'Stretch out your hand over the sea, and the waters shall return upon Mitsrayim, upon his chariotry and upon his horsemen'"). The verb וְיָשֻׁבוּ (ve-yashuvu) = "shall return" — from שׁוב (shuv, "to return"). The same verb used in v. 2 for Yisra'el's turning back (ve-yashuvu) now describes the waters' return. The waters go back to their place; Mitsrayim has no return. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν· ἔκτεινον τὴν χεῖρά σου ἐπὶ τὴν θάλασσαν, καὶ ἀποκαταστήτω τὸ ὕδωρ καὶ ἐπικαλυψάτω τοὺς Αἰγυπτίους, ἐπί τε τὰ ἅρματα καὶ τοὺς ἀναβάτας — ἀποκαθίστημι ("restore") for yashuvu.

**27** And Mosheh stretched out [vayyet] his hand over the sea, and the sea returned [vayashov] at the turning [lifnot] of the boqer [morning] to its steady-flow [le-eitano]; and Mitsrayim [was] fleeing [nasim] toward it, and YHWH shook off [vaye'na'er] Mitsrayim into the midst of the sea.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֵּט מֹשֶׁה אֶת־יָדוֹ עַל־הַיָּם וַיָּשָׁב הַיָּם לִפְנוֹת בֹּקֶר לְאֵיתָנוֹ וּמִצְרַיִם נָסִים לִקְרָאתוֹ וַיְנַעֵר יְהוָה אֶת־מִצְרַיִם בְּתוֹךְ הַיָּם (vayyet Mosheh et-yado al-ha-yam vayashov ha-yam lifnot boqer le-eitano u-Mitsrayim nasim liqrato vaye'na'er YHWH et-Mitsrayim be-tokh ha-yam, "and Mosheh stretched out his hand over the sea, and the sea returned at the turning of morning to its steady-flow; and Mitsrayim [was] fleeing toward it, and YHWH shook off Mitsrayim into the midst of the sea"). **Key terms:** The phrase לִפְנוֹת בֹּקֶר (lifnot boqer) = "at the turning of morning" — from פנה (panah, "to turn, face") — the moment when night turns to dawn. The word לְאֵיתָנוֹ (le-eitano) = "to its steady-flow / its perennial state / its strength" — from אֵיתָן (eitan, "perennial, enduring, strong"). The sea returns to its natural, perpetual condition — its eitan, its constant flow. The word is the same root as the name Eitan/Ethan, meaning "enduring." The verb נָסִים (nasim) = "fleeing" — participle of נוס (nus, "to flee"). Mitsrayim flees toward the returning waters — running into their own destruction. The phrase לִקְרָאתוֹ (liqrato) = "toward it / to meet it" — they flee directly into the path of the returning sea. The verb וַיְנַעֵר (vaye'na'er) = piel of נער (na'ar, "to shake, shake off") — "YHWH shook off Mitsrayim" — a violent image, like shaking insects from a garment or fruit from a tree. YHWH flings Egypt into the sea as one shakes off something clinging. LXX: ἐξέτεινεν δὲ Μωυσῆς τὴν χεῖρα ἐπὶ τὴν θάλασσαν, καὶ ἀπεκατέστη τὸ ὕδωρ πρὸς ἡμέραν ἐπὶ χώρας· οἱ δὲ Αἰγύπτιοι ἔφυγον ὑπὸ τὸ ὕδωρ, καὶ ἐξετίναξεν κύριος τοὺς Αἰγυπτίους μέσον τῆς θαλάσσης ("and Mosheh stretched out his hand over the sea, and the water was restored toward day to its place; and the Egyptians fled under the water, and the Lord shook out the Egyptians into the midst of the sea") — ἐκτινάσσω ("shake out") for vaye'na'er.

**28** And the waters returned [vayashuvu] and covered [vayekhassu] the chariotry [ha-rekhev] and the horsemen [ha-parashim] — all the army [kheil] of Par'oh, the ones coming in after them into the sea; there did not remain [lo-nish'ar] among them even one [ad-ekhad].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיָּשֻׁבוּ הַמַּיִם וַיְכַסּוּ אֶת־הָרֶכֶב וְאֶת־הַפָּרָשִׁים לְכֹל חֵיל פַּרְעֹה הַבָּאִים אַחֲרֵיהֶם בַּיָּם לֹא נִשְׁאַר בָּהֶם עַד־אֶחָד (vayashuvu ha-mayim vayekhassu et-ha-rekhev ve-et-ha-parashim le-khol kheil Par'oh ha-ba'im achareihem ba-yam lo nish'ar bahem ad-ekhad, "and the waters returned and covered the chariotry and the horsemen — all the army of Par'oh, the ones coming in after them into the sea; there did not remain among them even one"). **Key terms:** The verb וַיְכַסּוּ (vayekhassu) = piel of כסה (kasah, "to cover") — the waters "covered" the army, total submersion. The phrase לֹא נִשְׁאַר בָּהֶם עַד־אֶחָד (lo nish'ar bahem ad-ekhad) = "there did not remain among them even one" — from שׁאר (sha'ar, "to remain, be left over"). Complete annihilation: not a single survivor from the force that entered the sea. The number echoes the completeness of the tenth plague — every firstborn, every soldier. LXX: καὶ ἐπαναστραφὲν τὸ ὕδωρ ἐκάλυψεν τὰ ἅρματα καὶ τοὺς ἀναβάτας καὶ πᾶσαν τὴν δύναμιν Φαραω τοὺς εἰσπεπορευμένους ὀπίσω αὐτῶν εἰς τὴν θάλασσαν· οὐ κατελείφθη ἐξ αὐτῶν οὐδὲ εἷς ("and the water returning covered the chariots and the riders and all the force of Pharao that had entered after them into the sea; there was not left of them not even one").

## Yisra'el Saved — The Great Seeing (vv. 29–31)

**29** And the sons of Yisra'el walked [halekhu] on the yabbashah [dry ground] in the midst of the sea; and the waters [were] to them a khomah [wall] on their right [mi-yeminam] and on their left [u-mi-semolam].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּבְנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל הָלְכוּ בַיַּבָּשָׁה בְּתוֹךְ הַיָּם וְהַמַּיִם לָהֶם חֹמָה מִימִינָם וּמִשְּׂמֹאלָם (u-venei Yisra'el halekhu va-yabbashah be-tokh ha-yam ve-ha-mayim lahem khomah mi-yeminam u-mi-semolam, "and the sons of Yisra'el walked on the dry ground in the midst of the sea; and the waters [were] to them a wall on their right and on their left"). This verse nearly repeats v. 22 but with a crucial shift: in v. 22 they "went into" (vayavo'u) the sea; here they "walked" (halekhu) through it — the passage is complete. The repetition frames the entire Egyptian destruction (vv. 23–28) between two statements of Yisra'el's safe passage, creating a literary envelope. The walls of water that destroyed Egypt protected Yisra'el — the same water, two opposite fates. LXX: οἱ δὲ υἱοὶ Ισραηλ ἐπορεύθησαν διὰ ξηρᾶς ἐν μέσῳ τῆς θαλάσσης· τὸ δὲ ὕδωρ αὐτοῖς τεῖχος ἐκ δεξιῶν καὶ τεῖχος ἐξ εὐωνύμων.

**30** And YHWH saved [vayosha] Yisra'el on that day from the hand [mi-yad] of Mitsrayim; and Yisra'el saw Mitsrayim dead [met] upon the lip [sefat] of the sea.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיּוֹשַׁע יְהוָה בַּיּוֹם הַהוּא אֶת־יִשְׂרָאֵל מִיַּד מִצְרָיִם וַיַּרְא יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־מִצְרַיִם מֵת עַל־שְׂפַת הַיָּם (vayosha YHWH ba-yom ha-hu et-Yisra'el mi-yad Mitsrayim vayar Yisra'el et-Mitsrayim met al-sefat ha-yam, "and YHWH saved Yisra'el on that day from the hand of Mitsrayim; and Yisra'el saw Mitsrayim dead upon the lip of the sea"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — salvation realized and seen:** The verb וַיּוֹשַׁע (vayosha) = hiphil of ישׁע (yasha, "to save, deliver") — "He saved." The root of yeshu'ah (v. 13) — the salvation Mosheh promised is now accomplished. The phrase מִיַּד מִצְרָיִם (mi-yad Mitsrayim) = "from the hand of Mitsrayim" — the hand (yad) imagery runs throughout: YHWH's strong hand, Yisra'el's high hand, Mosheh's outstretched hand, and now deliverance from Mitsrayim's hand. The verb וַיַּרְא (vayar) = "and [Yisra'el] saw" — from ראה (ra'ah, "to see"). Mosheh commanded "see!" (re'u) in v. 13; now they see. The seeing is visceral: מֵת (met) = "dead" — singular collective, the corpses of the Egyptian army. The word שְׂפָה (sefah) = "lip, edge, shore" — the bodies wash up on the shoreline. The shore is the "lip" of the sea, the boundary between water and land, where the dead of Mitsrayim are deposited as visible proof of YHWH's victory. LXX: καὶ ἐρρύσατο κύριος τὸν Ισραηλ ἐν τῇ ἡμέρᾳ ἐκείνῃ ἐκ χειρὸς τῶν Αἰγυπτίων· καὶ εἶδεν Ισραηλ τοὺς Αἰγυπτίους τεθνηκότας παρὰ τὸ χεῖλος τῆς θαλάσσης — ῥύομαι ("rescue, deliver") for vayosha; χεῖλος ("lip") for sefat.

**31** And Yisra'el saw [vayar] the yad ha-gedolah [great hand] which YHWH did [asah] against Mitsrayim; and the people feared [vayire'u] YHWH, and they believed [vaya'aminu] in YHWH and in Mosheh His servant [avdo].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיַּרְא יִשְׂרָאֵל אֶת־הַיָּד הַגְּדֹלָה אֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה יְהוָה בְּמִצְרַיִם וַיִּירְאוּ הָעָם אֶת־יְהוָה וַיַּאֲמִינוּ בַּיהוָה וּבְמֹשֶׁה עַבְדּוֹ (vayar Yisra'el et-ha-yad ha-gedolah asher asah YHWH be-Mitsrayim vayire'u ha-am et-YHWH vaya'aminu ba-YHWH u-ve-Mosheh avdo, "and Yisra'el saw the great hand which YHWH did against Mitsrayim; and the people feared YHWH, and they believed in YHWH and in Mosheh His servant"). **THEOLOGICALLY CRITICAL — the climactic verse of the sea narrative:** The verb וַיַּרְא (vayar) = "saw" — the third occurrence of ראה in the conclusion (vv. 30–31). Seeing dominates: they see the dead (v. 30), they see the great hand (v. 31). The phrase הַיָּד הַגְּדֹלָה (ha-yad ha-gedolah) = "the great hand" — the definitive metaphor for divine power in the exodus. The hand of YHWH — the same hand that struck Egypt's firstborn, that parted the sea, that destroyed the army — is now "seen" by Yisra'el. The verb וַיִּירְאוּ (vayire'u) = "they feared" — from ירא (yare, "to fear, revere"). In v. 10, they feared (vayire'u) Mitsrayim; now they fear YHWH. The object of fear has shifted: from human oppressor to divine liberator. The fear of YHWH replaces the fear of Par'oh. The verb וַיַּאֲמִינוּ (vaya'aminu) = hiphil of אמן (aman, "to be firm, reliable, trustworthy") — "they believed, they trusted." The root אמן gives us "amen" and "emunah" (faithfulness). To believe (he'emin) is to consider reliable, to trust, to place one's confidence in. The preposition בְּ (be) = "in" — they believed in YHWH and in Mosheh. The phrase עַבְדּוֹ (avdo) = "His servant" — from עֶבֶד (eved, "servant, slave"). Mosheh is YHWH's eved — the same word that described Israel's slavery in Egypt (avadim) now describes Mosheh's honored role as YHWH's servant. The eved of YHWH is the opposite of the eved of Par'oh. This verse is the theological climax of the entire plague-and-exodus narrative: the people see, fear, and believe. The sequence is experiential: sight leads to reverence, reverence leads to faith. What was commanded in v. 13 ("stand and see the salvation of YHWH") is fulfilled in v. 31 ("Yisra'el saw the great hand"). LXX: εἶδεν δὲ Ισραηλ τὴν χεῖρα τὴν μεγάλην, ἃ ἐποίησεν κύριος τοῖς Αἰγυπτίοις· ἐφοβήθη δὲ ὁ λαὸς τὸν κύριον καὶ ἐπίστευσαν τῷ θεῷ καὶ Μωυσῇ τῷ θεράποντι αὐτοῦ ("and Israel saw the great hand, which things the Lord did to the Egyptians; and the people feared the Lord and believed God and Moses His attendant") — **textual note:** LXX reads θεράπων ("attendant, minister") for eved/avdo — a term of higher honor than δοῦλος ("slave"), suggesting a trusted household servant or minister rather than a slave.

## Key Lexical Notes — Exodus 14

- **beyad ramah** (בְּיָד רָמָה, v. 8): "with a high/uplifted hand" — the gesture of triumph, defiance, and bold departure. Yisra'el goes out not as fugitives but as the liberated, hand raised high. The image recurs in Numbers 15:30 and 33:3. The "high hand" is the opposite of the bowed head of slavery.

- **yeshu'ah** (יְשׁוּעָה, v. 13): "salvation, deliverance" — from ישׁע (yasha, "to save"). The noun denotes the act of divine rescue. This is not human victory but YHWH's saving deed. The word becomes central to Israelite theology — the God who saves (moshia) and the salvation (yeshu'ah) He provides. The name Yehoshua/Yeshua/Jesus derives from this root.

- **baqa** (בקע, vv. 16, 21): "to split, cleave, divide" — the definitive verb of the sea crossing. The splitting of the sea is an act of cosmic power — the verb is used for splitting rocks (Isa 48:21), splitting the earth (Num 16:31), and the primordial separation of waters. The baqa of the sea recapitulates creation's ordering of chaos.

- **ruakh qadim azzah** (רוּחַ קָדִים עַזָּה, v. 21): "a strong east wind" — the natural instrument of the supernatural miracle. The east wind (qadim) is the desert wind — hot, desiccating, powerful. YHWH uses a natural force (wind) to accomplish an unprecedented act (splitting the sea). The ruakh (wind/spirit) of God that hovered over the waters in creation now drives them apart.

- **khomah** (חוֹמָה, vv. 22, 29): "wall" — the waters stand vertically like fortification walls. The image is architectural and military: Yisra'el passes through a corridor flanked by walls of water. The same walls that protect Yisra'el will collapse upon Egypt.

- **vayashqef** (וַיַּשְׁקֵף, v. 24): "He looked down" — from שׁקף (shaqaf, "to look out, peer, gaze down from above"). The divine gaze from the pillar of fire and cloud — YHWH looks down upon the Egyptian army, and His looking is their undoing. The verb implies looking from an elevated position through a window or opening.

- **vaye'na'er** (וַיְנַעֵר, v. 27): "He shook off" — from נער (na'ar, "to shake"). YHWH shakes Mitsrayim off like a garment shaken free of dust or a tree shaken to release fruit. The image is of casual, overwhelming power — what is terrifying to Egypt is effortless to YHWH.

- **vaya'aminu** (וַיַּאֲמִינוּ, v. 31): "they believed" — hiphil of אמן (aman, "to be firm"). The culminating verb of the sea narrative. Faith arises from sight: they saw the great hand, feared YHWH, and believed. The root אמן carries connotations of firmness, reliability, and trust — to believe is to regard as firm and trustworthy. This is the first time the collective Yisra'el is said to "believe" in YHWH.
